sections.content:
Planning Gets You There

The best way to get into your selling groove is to start with a territory or account plan.

Your account or territory can be defined by:
  • Segment—either Enterprise (EBU), Commercial (CMRL), or Emerging Small to Mid-sized Business (ESMB) accounts, but never a mix.
  • Geographic Region—like India or the UK, for example.
  • Industry—like retail or financial services, for example.
  • Geography and Industry—sometimes your territory is a hybrid.
Don’t be alarmed if you hear teammates or leaders also talk about patches. A patch is the same as a territory. We prefer to refer to it as territory here, as it’s a more official term.

Sure, all of this sounds obvious and somewhat simple, but let’s face it: Making a plan and keeping everything on track is hard work. So, we have some tools for you to kick-start your planning and keep everything organized:
  • Quota Attainment Plan (QAP): The base template you use to organize your customers and prospects, prioritize your goals, and collaborate with your leader and extended team.
  • Standard Account Plan Template: A supplementary template you use to focus on your customer’s priorities, background, and potential.
  • Strategic Account Plan Template: A supplementary template you use to focus on your customer’s priorities and define your execution plan.
These tools are updated throughout the year, and are designed for you and your team to sell better, together.


Time for Tiering

Let’s talk about tiering. What’s tiering? It’s the method you use with your team to divide up customers and prospects among account executives (AEs), business development reps (BDRs), sales development reps (SDRs), and the marketing team.
We use tiering in order to manage our territories and accounts with the best support and resources, determine how committed each customer or prospect is to working with Salesforce, and use that information to use our time wisely.
There are four tiers in our Salesforce tiering model:

TierType of CustomerRole
Tier 1High-priority customers and prospectsAccount executives (AEs)
Tier 2Existing opportunities and lower-potential customersBusiness development reps (BDRs)
Tier 3Prospects with potentialBDRs and sales development reps (SDRs)
Unknown prospectsMarketing

As you can see:
  • Account executives (AEs) work with high-potential accounts and prospects.
  • Business development reps (BDRs) develop potential prospects and maintain relationships with existing, low-potential customers.
  • Sales development reps (SDRs) qualify leads who show interest and who contact Salesforce directly.
  • Marketing supports everyone with campaigns for new prospects.
Even though you’re ultimately responsible for delivering success in your territory and for your accounts, you don’t do it alone. You have a team to help you with everything from the heavy lifting to focusing on the finer details. Collaborating with your team is key to creating and maintaining your plan.


Account Types and Tools

Let’s explore how we organize accounts within tiers.
If you’re an AE, you’re part of Tier 1, and you focus on working with high-priority customers and prospects. We organize all of these customers and prospects into these account types:
Tier 1 Account TypeDescription
Territory


Opportunistic Account and Opportunity Management—or a number of accounts defined by a specific segment, geography, or industry. AEs in this category, also known as geo reps, handle more than three accounts.
Strategic AccountsHigh-potential accounts managed by dedicated AEs. AEs in this category handle up to three accounts each and focus on driving annual contract value (ACV).
IB50 AccountsThe 50 largest-by-revenue, install base (IB) accounts recognized as trendsetters in their industries. Install base accounts are accounts with licenses that are currently in use.
Top 100


Accounts that have been customers for multiple years and are currently following a multi-year plan with us, including:
  • Global account manager (GAM) accounts—single-customer EBU accounts that have global footprints and centralized buying and decision-making structures.
  • Go for the Sky (GFTS) accounts—high-potential EBU accounts where the potential is a minimum of $5 million USD over the course of 18 months.


As you move up the pyramid, the account types get more specific. Here’s how we describe each account type.
You might be wondering: What’s the difference between territory and strategic accounts? It’s a matter of specificity. AEs with territory accounts work with 10 to 20—or many more—accounts in a specific segment, geographic region, or industry. AEs with strategic accounts typically work with only one to three customers.
Of course, it’s great to understand the differences between all of these territory and account types, but the only type that’s really important for you is yours.
Now, it's time to connect the dots. We use certain planning tools to work with territories and accounts, and we organize them based on account types. 
This table shows the plans you’ll use in each territory or account.

Territory or Account TypeWhich Plan or Plans You Use
Top 100 - GFTS accountsQAP and Strategic Account Plan
Top 100 - GAM accountsStrategic Account Plan
IB50 accountsQAP and Standard Account Plan
Strategic accountsQAP and Standard Account Plan
TerritoryQAP
Did you notice? Unless you’re a global account manager (GAM), everyone fills out the QAP. We’ll get to that, but first, let’s talk about timelines and processes. See you in a flash!
